title: [Question] How to start adb (android debug bridge) from inside a podman container?
body:
**Description**

I have been exploring podman since the last couple of days. I tried to run adb from inside a podman container. And it just fails to start the adb server.
Please note, it works if I add the `--privileged` flag. But I have been trying to avoid using that flag unless there's no choice.
Apologies if this is the wrong place for this question.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

These steps work for docker. I am able to see my attached android device.

```
sudo docker run --rm -it --device=/dev/bus -v /dev/serial:/dev/serial:ro --cap-add SYS_PTRACE ubuntu:20.04
apt update && apt install -y android-tools-adb android-tools-fastboot
adb devices
```

Output:
```
List of devices attached
* daemon not running; starting now at tcp:5037
* daemon started successfully
<my_device_id>	device
```

But when I do the same with podman

```
podman run --rm -it --device=/dev/bus -v /dev/serial:/dev/serial:ro --cap-add SYS_PTRACE ubuntu:20.04
apt update && apt install -y android-tools-adb android-tools-fastboot
adb devices
```

Output:
```
List of devices attached
* daemon not running; starting now at tcp:5037
ADB server didn't ACK
Full server startup log: /tmp/adb.0.log
Server had pid: 1184
--- adb starting (pid 1184) ---
adb I 12-05 17:05:45  1184  1184 main.cpp:57] Android Debug Bridge version 1.0.39
adb I 12-05 17:05:45  1184  1184 main.cpp:57] Version 1:8.1.0+r23-5ubuntu2
adb I 12-05 17:05:45  1184  1184 main.cpp:57] Installed as /usr/lib/android-sdk/platform-tools/adb
adb I 12-05 17:05:45  1184  1184 main.cpp:57] 
adb I 12-05 17:05:45  1184  1184 adb_auth_host.cpp:416] adb_auth_init...
adb I 12-05 17:05:45  1184  1184 adb_auth_host.cpp:262] User key '/root/.android/adbkey' does not exist...
adb I 12-05 17:05:45  1184  1184 adb_auth_host.cpp:109] generate_key(/root/.android/adbkey)...
adb I 12-05 17:05:45  1184  1184 adb_auth_host.cpp:77] write_public_keyfile...
adb I 12-05 17:05:45  1184  1184 adb_auth_host.cpp:56] get_user_info...
adb I 12-05 17:05:45  1184  1184 adb_auth_host.cpp:174] read_key_file '/root/.android/adbkey'...
adb I 12-05 17:05:45  1184  1184 adb_auth_host.cpp:391] adb_auth_inotify_init...

* failed to start daemon
error: cannot connect to daemon
```

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      2.2.0
API Version:  2.1.0
Go Version:   go1.15.2
Built:        Thu Jan  1 05:30:00 1970
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

comments.body: Does it work with the regular `sudo podman`, instead of podman rootless ?
No, it doesn't work with `sudo podman` either.

Some more info, running `adb nodaemon server` inside podman (both sudo and rootless) shows a segmentation fault. But it works fine with docker.

```
# adb nodaemon server
adb I 12-06 09:55:47    12    12 auth.cpp:437] adb_auth_init...
adb I 12-06 09:55:47    12    12 auth.cpp:262] User key '/root/.android/adbkey' does not exist...
adb I 12-06 09:55:47    12    12 auth.cpp:97] generate_key(/root/.android/adbkey)...
Segmentation fault (core dumped)
```

Can you try adding the '--privileged' flag? Some of our security defaults
are tougher than Docker, so if it works with them disabled that is most
likely the cause.

On Sun, Dec 6, 2020, 04:57 Jeet Parekh <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> Some more info, running adb nodaemon server inside podman (both sudo and
> rootless) shows a segmentation fault. But it works fine with docker.
>
> # adb nodaemon server
> adb I 12-06 09:55:47    12    12 auth.cpp:437] adb_auth_init...
> adb I 12-06 09:55:47    12    12 auth.cpp:262] User key '/root/.android/adbkey' does not exist...
> adb I 12-06 09:55:47    12    12 auth.cpp:97] generate_key(/root/.android/adbkey)...
> Segmentation fault (core dumped)
>
> —
> You are receiving this because you are subscribed to this thread.
> Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub
> <https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/8610#issuecomment-739480213>,
> or unsubscribe
> <https://github.com/notifications/unsubscribe-auth/AB3AOCHWRVF7U4DXMY6FNRTSTNIRJANCNFSM4UOPYFTQ>
> .
>

Adding the `--privileged` flag works. But it does not detect attached devices in rootless mode. You have to use `--privileged` + `sudo` to have adb detect the attached devices. I mentioned this in the issue description. I was exploring and trying to figure out how to avoid using `--privileged`.

That could definitely be the cause. I'm curious what's exactly happening here. And is there a way to make it work without `--privileged`...? Like it works with docker.

Hm. So it works as rootless with the `--privileged` flag, and you'd like to turn it off?

Security-wise, `--privileged` has three effects: it enables all capabilities (`--cap-add ALL`), disabled Seccomp filtering (`--security-opt seccomp=unconfined`), and (on Apparmor distros) disables Apparmor (`--security-opt apparmor=unconfined`). Can you try each of those options individually without `--privileged` and see if any of them do the trick? We can drill down further once we know exactly what is doing the blocking.

> So it works as rootless with the `--privileged` flag, and you'd like to turn it off?

adb works, but it doesn't detect the devices. So, it's kinda useless. You have to use sudo + privileged to have adb work as well as detect the devices.

Ideally, it would be good to have it work without sudo or privileged. But again, I am just exploring to see if it's possible.

Before testing those three options, I'll note the current behaviour of two commands.

1. adb server doesn't start. A [segmentation fault](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/8610#issuecomment-739480213) happens.
```
podman run --rm -it --device=/dev/bus -v /dev/serial:/dev/serial:ro --cap-add SYS_PTRACE ubuntu:20.04
```

2. adb server starts, but cannot detect the attached android device. If you run this command with sudo, then it detects the devices.
```
podman run --rm -it --device=/dev/bus -v /dev/serial:/dev/serial:ro --privileged ubuntu:20.04
```

3. For all of these three commands, just like (1), adb doesn't start. And a segmentation fault happens. I tried these three commands both rootless, and with sudo.
```
podman run --rm -it --device=/dev/bus -v /dev/serial:/dev/serial:ro --cap-add ALL ubuntu:20.04
podman run --rm -it --device=/dev/bus -v /dev/serial:/dev/serial:ro --security-opt seccomp=unconfined ubuntu:20.04
podman run --rm -it --device=/dev/bus -v /dev/serial:/dev/serial:ro --security-opt apparmor=unconfined ubuntu:20.04
```

Running without `sudo` may be impossible, given permissions issues. I presume you were running `docker` as root (perhaps with your user added to the `docker` group so you could access it without `sudo`, but the daemon was running as root) - so full permissions were available. Rootless Podman is not SUID, and has no elevated privileges - so my first impression would be that we don't have permission to access the devices you're mounting into the container.

> I presume you were running docker as root (perhaps with your user added to the docker group so you could access it without sudo, but the daemon was running as root) - so full permissions were available.

Right. I am running docker as root. Not by adding the user to the docker group. Explicitly with sudo.

> so my first impression would be that we don't have permission to access the devices you're mounting into the container

Makes sense. I just tried a plain rootless `podman run --rm -it ubuntu:20.04` without any device or volume mount. And the adb server was able to start without a segmentation fault.

Do you think it's possible to make it work with root, but without privileged?

I can't see a reason why sudo, but no `--privileged`, could not be made to work. It is very interesting that it doesn't seem to be security, though. Potentially could be a device not being mounted in from the host, I suppose. @giuseppe @rhatdan Any thoughts here?

As a rootless user can you access these devices?  If not, then it will not be able to be done in rootless mode.  The devices probably need to be readable and perhaps writeable by the user.  If they are protected by root, then it probably impossible to use rootless.

--privileged also unmasks all of /proc and /sys, which might be why you cannot use these devices without being root.
In the main branch of podman we just added a new feature, which allows you to disable the masking

podman run --security-opt unmask=all ...

Which should remove the masks.

> As a rootless user can you access these devices?

Yes. If I run `adb devices` directly on my laptop without `sudo`, then it works just fine. It lists my devices.
My user does have root privileges though. But it works fine without `sudo`. Not sure if that matters.

> In the main branch of podman we just added a new feature, which allows you to disable the masking.

I guess I could try building podman from the main branch and try adb again. I wasn't able to find any build steps though.

For me, it is the usb claim that fails:

```
[pid  1274] ioctl(11, USBDEVFS_CLAIMINTERFACE, 0x7fa4791efa6c) = 0
[pid  1274] --- SIGSEGV {si_signo=SIGSEGV, si_code=SEGV_MAPERR, si_addr=0x4} ---
```

When running on the host, it succeeds.


The other difference is that Docker mounts the entire directory, while Podman mounts individual devices (without cgroup perm)

Docker
```
            "Devices": [
                {
                    "PathOnHost": "/dev/bus",
                    "PathInContainer": "/dev/bus",
                    "CgroupPermissions": "rwm"
                }
            ],
```

Podman
```
           "Devices": [
                {
                    "PathOnHost": "/dev/bus/usb/001/001",
                    "PathInContainer": "/dev/bus/usb/001/001",
                    "CgroupPermissions": ""
                },
                ... etc etc ....
```

This was without --privileged (with sudo)
Look like the cgrouppermissions is definitely  a bug,   Not sure about mounting all of the devices.
@giuseppe WDYT?

I use the dlv to debug this, I think the problem should be in the part of listening to the container creation event and creating the container
```
(dlv) p ctr.config.Spec.Linux.Resources.Devices
[]github.com/opencontainers/runtime-spec/specs-go.LinuxDeviceCgroup len: 2, cap: 2, [
	{Allow: true, Type: "c", Major: *189, Minor: *0, Access: "rwm"},
	{Allow: true, Type: "c", Major: *189, Minor: *1, Access: "rwm"},
]
(dlv) ls
> github.com/containers/podman/v2/libpod.(*BoltState).AddContainer() libpod/boltdb_state.go:688 (PC: 0x1a51991)
   683:	
   684:		if ctr.config.Pod != "" {
   685:			return errors.Wrapf(define.ErrInvalidArg, "cannot add a container that belongs to a pod with AddContainer - use AddContainerToPod")
   686:		}
   687:	
=> 688:		return s.addContainer(ctr, nil)
   689:	}
   690:	
   691:	// RemoveContainer removes a container from the state
   692:	// Only removes containers not in pods - for containers that are a member of a
   693:	// pod, use RemoveContainerFromPod
(dlv) p ctr.config.Spec.Linux.Devices
[]github.com/opencontainers/runtime-spec/specs-go.LinuxDevice len: 2, cap: 2, [
	{
		Path: "/dev/bus/usb/001/001",
		Type: "c",
		Major: 189,
		Minor: 0,
		FileMode: *8628,
		UID: *0,
		GID: *0,},
	{
		Path: "/dev/bus/usb/001/002",
		Type: "c",
		Major: 189,
		Minor: 1,
		FileMode: *8628,
		UID: *0,
		GID: *0,},
]
(dlv) 

```

```
[root@Centos8 /sys/fs/cgroup/devices/machine.slice/libpod-d993bbcab954e98d233f59867ae25226b1fa46c1359a42fa23b9a3d422a8f94b.scope]# podman inspect t1 | grep -i pid
            "Pid": 328791,
            "ConmonPid": 328780,
        "ConmonPidFile": "/var/run/containers/storage/overlay-containers/d993bbcab954e98d233f59867ae25226b1fa46c1359a42fa23b9a3d422a8f94b/userdata/conmon.pid",
            "PidMode": "private",
            "PidsLimit": 2048,
[root@Centos8 /sys/fs/cgroup/devices/machine.slice/libpod-d993bbcab954e98d233f59867ae25226b1fa46c1359a42fa23b9a3d422a8f94b.scope]# cat notify_on_release 
0
[root@Centos8 /sys/fs/cgroup/devices/machine.slice/libpod-d993bbcab954e98d233f59867ae25226b1fa46c1359a42fa23b9a3d422a8f94b.scope]# cat cgroup.procs 
328791
[root@Centos8 /sys/fs/cgroup/devices/machine.slice/libpod-d993bbcab954e98d233f59867ae25226b1fa46c1359a42fa23b9a3d422a8f94b.scope]# cat cgroup.clone_children 
0
[root@Centos8 /sys/fs/cgroup/devices/machine.slice/libpod-d993bbcab954e98d233f59867ae25226b1fa46c1359a42fa23b9a3d422a8f94b.scope]# cat devices.list 
c 189:1 rwm
c 189:0 rwm
c 136:* rwm
c 5:2 rwm
c 5:0 rwm
c 1:9 rwm
c 1:8 rwm
c 1:7 rwm
c 1:5 rwm
c 1:3 rwm
b *:* m
c *:* m
c 10:200 rwm
```
look like correct 
```
c 189:1 rwm
c 189:0 rwm

```

as rootless could you try with `-v /dev:/dev --mount type=devpts,destination=/dev/pts`?

The cgroup settings don't affect rootless as anyway a rootless user cannot configure the devices controller both on cgroup v1 and cgroup v2.

This fails.

```
podman run --rm -it -v /dev:/dev --mount type=devpts,destination=/dev/pts ubuntu:20.04

Error: container_linux.go:370: starting container process caused: process_linux.go:459: container init caused: open /dev/console: permission denied: OCI permission denied
```

This works fine. But trying to run adb still causes a segmentation fault.

```
podman run --rm -it -v /dev/bus:/dev/bus --mount type=devpts,destination=/dev/pts ubuntu:20.04
```
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Any further update on this issue?
For visibility, maybe change the label from question to bug?
I see the label has been changed to question again. Perhaps change the title to something describing the bug uncovered in this discussion, and re-label it as bug...?
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
I am not sure what bug has been descoved in this discussion, Could someone clarify what they think needs to happen to close this issue?
please try: `podman run --rm -it -v /dev:/dev --mount type=devpts,destination=/dev/pts ...` with crun instead of runc

I believe this got fixed in the last four months. adb inside a podman container can now detect my android device.

@rhatdan, https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/8610#issuecomment-739956160
> Look like the cgrouppermissions is definitely a bug, Not sure about mounting all of the devices.

@giuseppe, both of these work now. Segmentation fault does not happen. And I can access the android device via adb.

```bash
podman --runtime=/usr/bin/crun run --rm -it -v /dev/bus/usb/:/dev/bus/usb ubuntu:20.04
podman --runtime=/usr/bin/runc run --rm -it -v /dev/bus/usb/:/dev/bus/usb ubuntu:20.04
```
thanks, so are you fine if we close this issue?
